rule_text: When no return-plane geometry is available, narrow PCB traces can be approximated in the high single-digit nH/cm range as a first-order self-inductance estimate.
use_case: Initial LTspice sensitivity sweep for conducted EMI and switching current paths when only trace length is known.
limitations: Actual inductance depends strongly on return path, plane distance, trace width and loop geometry.
Pre-compliance estimate only; not a measured or extracted PCB value.
Use geometry-based calculation when stack-up and layout are available.
recommended_action: Use as a min/typ/max sweep band, not as a fixed extracted PCB value.
simulation_usage: Create .step values for the series trace inductance in LTspice and observe V(MEAS) sensitivity.

rule_text: Trace self-inductance grows approximately in proportion to trace length (with a weak logarithmic term from width), so a critical trace's inductive contribution scales roughly linearly with how long it is.
use_case: Deciding which traces to shorten first and how to scale a per-cm inductance rule to a specific net.
limitations: Linear scaling is first-order; mutual coupling and return-path changes along the route are ignored.
Not valid once the trace becomes electrically long relative to the highest frequency of interest.
recommended_action: Scale the per-cm rule-of-thumb by measured trace length and keep a min/typ/max band; prioritise shortening the longest fast-edge traces.
simulation_usage: Sweep inductance proportional to candidate trace-length values to bound the EMI impact of a layout change.

rule_text: A typical signal or power via in a standard-thickness board contributes a first-order series inductance of order one nanohenry; a common rule-of-thumb is roughly 1 nH per via.
use_case: First-order budgeting of via inductance in decoupling and switching-loop paths before layout extraction.
limitations: Depends on board thickness, drill diameter, antipad and whether vias are paralleled.
Parallel vias reduce the effective inductance (roughly halved for a pair).
Pad and discontinuity inductance are not included.
recommended_action: Use the band for a sweep; add parallel vias as a candidate mitigation and re-estimate.
simulation_usage: Add a series L per via in LTspice and .step it across the min/typ/max band.

rule_text: The inductance of a current loop is driven by its enclosed area: a larger loop encloses more flux and presents higher loop inductance and stronger inductive coupling, which raises conducted and radiated EMI.
use_case: Explaining why tight switching loops and close return paths reduce EMI risk in DC/DC converters.
limitations: Loop inductance is strongly geometry-dependent; no single value applies without layout.
Reducing risk is not a guarantee of EMC compliance.
Partial-inductance vs loop-inductance distinction matters for accurate analysis.
recommended_action: Flag large switching loops as a layout risk; recommend minimising loop area and report the change as a risk reduction, not a pass.
simulation_usage: Compare LTspice variants with low vs high loop-inductance bands to bound the EMI delta.

rule_text: Parasitic inductance in a fast-switching current path produces a voltage disturbance V = L * di/dt; even a few nanohenries with a fast switching edge can create a volt-scale spike.
use_case: Quantifying why parasitic inductance on switching nodes and supply rails matters for conducted EMI.
limitations: di/dt depends on the switching device and gate drive and must be estimated, not assumed.
First-order lumped view; distributed and resonant effects are ignored.
Indicates EMI risk magnitude only; not a compliance verdict.
recommended_action: Combine a trace/via inductance band with an estimated di/dt range to bound the disturbance voltage; treat the result as a hypothesis to verify.
simulation_usage: Use LTspice transient results to read di/dt on switching nets and cross-check against L * di/dt with the parasitic-L sweep.

rule_text: Polygon-to-plane capacitance can be approximated to first order as a parallel-plate capacitor C = eps0 * eps_r * A / d, using a typical FR-4 relative permittivity around 4.3.
use_case: First-order estimate of plane/polygon coupling capacitance when only copper area and dielectric thickness are known.
limitations: Fringe-field capacitance and frequency dependence of eps_r are ignored.
Dielectric thickness must come from the stack-up; assume a default profile and flag low confidence if missing.
Parallel-plate model overestimates for small or narrow polygons.
recommended_action: Use the eps_r band and the parallel-plate formula for a min/typ/max capacitance; replace with a field solver when the stack-up is known.
simulation_usage: Add a shunt C between polygon and plane in LTspice and .step it across the resulting band.

rule_text: When the return-path geometry (proximity and continuity of a reference plane) is unknown, trace inductance and capacitance estimates carry large uncertainty and should be treated as low-to-medium confidence with a deliberately wide band.
use_case: Setting confidence and band width for parasitic estimates produced without layout or stack-up data.
limitations: A missing return path can change inductance by more than an order of magnitude.
This rule governs uncertainty handling; it provides no numeric parasitic value itself.
recommended_action: Flag the missing return-path data as a limitation, widen the min/typ/max band, and lower the reported confidence accordingly.
simulation_usage: Use the widened band as the .step range so the variant ranking reflects the return-path uncertainty.

rule_text: Parasitic estimates must be expressed as a min/typ/max range, never as a single value presented as certain, because rule-of-thumb geometry and material assumptions are inherently uncertain.
use_case: Governing how every per-net parasitic estimate is reported by the parasitics agent.
limitations: A range is still an estimate, not an extracted or measured value.
Bands must be justified by stated assumptions, not chosen arbitrarily.
recommended_action: Convert any single example value from a source into a bounded engineering rule with explicit limitations.
simulation_usage: Feed the min/typ/max band into LTspice .step sweeps rather than a single .param.

rule_text: When a parasitic value is unknown, prefer a parameter sweep over a single simulation so the analysis quantifies sensitivity instead of asserting one outcome.
use_case: Deciding how the parasitics agent and testbench treat uncertain trace/via/polygon parasitics.
limitations: Sweeps cost simulation time; choose a sensible number of corners.
A sweep bounds sensitivity but does not replace layout extraction.
recommended_action: Generate LTspice variants across the parasitic band and rank them, rather than committing to a single estimate.
simulation_usage: Emit .step directives for the uncertain parasitics and compare V(MEAS) across corners.

rule_text: A rule-of-thumb parasitic estimate is a placeholder: once trace geometry and stack-up are available it should be replaced with a geometry-based calculator, and for critical nets with a field solver or 3D extraction.
use_case: Defining the upgrade path from rule-of-thumb per-net estimates to extracted parasitics.
limitations: Calculators are still first-order; only 3D extraction captures full geometry and coupling.
Field-solver work is out of MVP scope and noted only as a future step.
recommended_action: When layout/stack-up exist, switch the parasitic value source from rule-of-thumb to a geometry-based calculator and flag remaining gaps.
simulation_usage: Replace rule-of-thumb .step bands with calculator-derived values once geometry is known.
